text: Runners who increased weekly running distance by 20-60% had significantly more injuries at 21 days compared to those who increased by less than 20%.
The risk difference for injury when increasing weekly distance by 20-60% versus less than 20% was 22.6% at 21 days into a training program.
The elevated injury risk from 20-60% weekly distance increases was significant at 21 days but not at 56 or 98 days, suggesting acute spikes in load are most dangerous in the short term.
specific_value: significant at 21 days, not at 56 or 98 days

text: Keeping weekly running distance increases below 20% is associated with lower injury risk during half-marathon preparation.
The running schedule followed prior to a sudden change in training load did not significantly modify the association between weekly distance change and injury risk.
In a 14-week half-marathon preparation period, 21.5% of recreational runners sustained a running-related injury.
